channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: As U.S.-Iran Cease-Fire Frays, Trump Faces a Muddled War and Unpalatable Options
item.description: The president appears to be confronting the consequences of a cease-fire deal cobbled together in haste, with little movement toward resolving the key issues driving the conflict.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump at the NATO summit in Ankara, Turkey, on Wednesday, where he declared the cease-fire with Iran “over” after less than a month.

item.title: Oil Market Calm Shattered by Fresh Hostilities Between US and Iran
item.description: Oil prices are likely to remain volatile as long as the United States and Iran are unable to resolve the status of the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Lisa Friedman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: License to Make Patriot Missiles Could Help Ukraine Defend Against Russia
item.description: With Patriot interceptors in short supply, President Trump’s statement that he would allow Ukraine to build them is a boon to Kyiv as it fights off Russian missile attacks. But it is just the start.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut, Nicholas Kulish and John Ismay
item.media:credit: Sergei Supinsky/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A Patriot air defense system in Ukraine, in 2024. President Volodymyr Zelensky has warned for weeks that his country was running out of interceptors for the system as Russia has stepped up attacks.

item.title: After Insulting Allies at NATO Summit, Trump Says They Love Him
item.description: The meeting in Turkey featured President Trump at his most mercurial, veering from scorn to praise for European countries, including some whose patience with mollifying him has worn thin.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager and Shawn McCreesh
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump at the NATO summit in Ankara, Turkey, on Wednesday.

item.title: Judge Orders $5 Million Trump Judgment Be Released to E. Jean Carroll
item.description: Ms. Carroll had asked a federal judge to order the president to pay the judgment after a jury found he sexually abused and defamed her.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle and Benjamin Weiser
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Manhattan jury awarded the multimillion-dollar judgment to the writer E. Jean Carroll in May 2023.

item.title: Democrats Grow Frustrated as Graham Platner Resists Dropping Out Quickly in Maine
item.description: Two days after a woman accused Graham Platner of rape, he had not given up his nomination for Senate in Maine, raising worries about whether and how his party might find a replacement.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner and Lisa Lerer
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: If Graham Platner ends his Senate bid, Maine Democrats face the daunting prospect of finding a new nominee in just weeks.

item.title: These Maine Democrats Might Replace Graham Platner in the Senate Race
item.description: Mr. Platner has not yet withdrawn from the race after a rape allegation, but Democrats in the state and nationally are already exploring potential alternatives.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: When Might Graham Platner Drop Out? Here’s the State of Play.
item.description: Here’s what we know about the uproar in the Democratic Party’s bid to unseat Senator Susan Collins.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Ryan David Brown for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner’s home in Sullivan, Maine

item.title: I Survived a Cold Plunge and All I Got Was Everything I Ever Wanted
item.description: I resisted the trend until I couldn’t any longer.
item.dc:creator: Taffy Brodesser-Akner
item.media:credit: Hannah Whitaker for The New York Times

item.title: Which Country Has the Best-Dressed World Cup Fans?
item.description: At World Cup matches featuring teams from five continents, our photographer captured fans, flags, fringe, face paint and at least one horned helmet.
item.dc:creator: Simbarashe Cha
item.media:credit: Photographs by Simbarashe Cha/The New York Times

item.title: 2026 Emmy Nominations: ‘The Pitt’ and ‘Hacks’ Lead the Pack
item.description: The HBO Max medical series earned 25 nominations in the drama category. And in comedy, the final season of “Hacks” set a record with 24 nominations.
item.dc:creator: John Koblin
item.category: Last Week Tonight With John Oliver (TV Program)

item.title: Emmy Nominees 2026: See the Full List
item.description: The 78th Emmy Awards ceremony is planned for Sept. 14. See all the nominees below.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez

item.title: Emmys Snubs and Surprises 2026: Jon Hamm, Ariana Madix and More
item.description: Other unexpected results include nods for Chase Infiniti and the “Love Island USA” host Ariana Madix and a snub for “The Amazing Race.”
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale
item.media:credit: Jessica Kourkounis/Apple TV+, via Associated Press
item.media:description: “Your Friends & Neighbors,” the Apple TV suburban satire, was a surprise nominee for best drama, but the star, Jon Hamm, was snubbed.

item.title: Vance, in Wisconsin, Highlights Twin Fights, Against Fraud and Democrats
item.description: The vice president called Democrats “a party that is fighting for fraud,” even as he highlighted the case of a woman who was first indicted under the Biden administration.
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.media:credit: Mark Schiefelbein/Agence France-Presse, via Pool/Afp Via Getty Images
item.media:description: “This is not about better or worse policy,” Vice President JD Vance told a crowd in Milwaukee. “This is about who is supporting fraudsters and who is supporting you.”

item.title: As Progressives Rack Up Wins, Hakeem Jeffries Shrugs Off Possible Challenges
item.description: In a wide-ranging interview with The New York Times, Hakeem Jeffries, the House Democratic leader, said he didn’t consider the recent wave of anti-establishment victories “an overall critique of the party.”
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni and Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times

item.title: Kentucky Gov. Andy Beshear Demands Health Update on Mitch McConnell
item.description: Senator Mitch McConnell has been hospitalized since June 14. Speculation about his health has spread in recent days.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Andy Beshear of Kentucky, middle, at a news conference in Lexington last month.

item.title: Family of Man Fatally Shot by ICE Agent Calls for Independent Inquiry
item.description: The man killed, Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, was mourned by his sons as a father, husband and business owner. Details of the encounter remain unclear.
item.dc:creator: Edgar Sandoval
item.media:credit: Antranik Tavitian/Reuters
item.media:description: Lorenzo Salgado Araujo’s family members gathered for news conference in Houston after he was fatally shot by federal agents.

item.title: What We Know About Immigration Officers Shooting at People in Vehicles
item.description: Federal agents have fired on at least 21 people, many in their cars, as part of President Trump’s deportation crackdown. At least five people were killed, including three U.S. citizens.
item.dc:creator: Allison McCann
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: Community members left flowers, candles, signs and the flag of Mexico at the site where an ICE agent shot Lorenzo Salgado Araujo during an encounter in Houston early Tuesday morning.

item.title: Judge Hannah C. Dugan Faces Fine But No Prison Time
item.description: The former Wisconsin judge, Hannah C. Dugan, was accused of ushering an undocumented immigrant out a side door as federal agents waited for him near her Milwaukee courtroom.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Lee Matz/Milwaukee Independent, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Hannah C. Dugan, a Wisconsin state judge, was found guilty of obstructing federal agents.

item.title: Conor McGregor Is Said to Have Continued Use of Banned Drugs After Injury Healed
item.description: Conor McGregor, who will make his return to fighting this weekend, took human growth hormone and anabolic steroids after recovering from a broken leg, people with knowledge of the matter said.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Schmidt
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Conor McGregor in Washington last year. He is scheduled to return to fight on Saturday for the first time since breaking his leg in 2021.

item.title: In San Francisco, Some Home Sellers Now Ask for OpenAI or Anthropic Stock
item.description: Even before OpenAI and Anthropic go public, they are distorting home sales in the San Francisco Bay Area, as people race to buy and sellers ask for stock instead of cash.
item.dc:creator: Emmy Martin
item.media:credit: Cayce Clifford for The New York Times
item.media:description: San Francisco home sales in May rose 12.2 percent from a year earlier, with the median price of a single-family home climbing to $2.14 million, according to a Homes.com report.

item.title: Fax and Telegram? New York’s Archaic Requirements Get an A.I. Overhaul.
item.description: Gov. Kathy Hochul will direct state agencies to eliminate or modernize antiquated requirements, and examine if task forces or councils have outlived their purpose.
item.dc:creator: Grace Ashford
item.media:credit: Cindy Schultz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Kathy Hochul said the Regulatory Reset initiative was designed to make government more efficient and work better for New Yorkers.

item.title: Midtown Neighborhood Has to Cope in a Troubled Building’s Shadow
item.description: Travelers were shut out of major hotels, and work ground to a halt for some on Tuesday after a building partially buckled in Manhattan.
item.dc:creator: Caitlyn Freeman
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: East 44th Street in Manhattan on Tuesday, as evacuations continued after a high-rise office building on 42nd Street was at risk of partial collapse.

item.title: How to Be a Good Guest at a Dinner Party
item.description: Tips for endearing yourself to your host and fellow attendees — and all but guaranteeing you’ll be asked back.
item.dc:creator: Rose Courteau

item.title: How to Cook With Flowers: 3 Recipes With Edible Blossoms
item.description: A trio of Philadelphia-based chefs share recipes for foods in which blossoms are more than mere garnish.
item.dc:creator: Luke Fortney

item.title: Can the United States Hold On?
item.description: Our July 4 bash was troubled, and America may go the way of the Ghaznavid Empire. But here are reasons to be more positive.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kristof
item.media:credit: Sam Gulliver for The New York Times

item.title: This Is One of the Hardest Jobs in America. Millions Are Doing It Alone.
item.description: Behind closed doors, millions of Americans are stepping into one of the hardest roles they’ll ever take on: caring for their aging parents.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Su, Emily Holzknecht and Jan Kobal

item.title: Decades Late, Museum Scrambles to Return 2,700 Native Remains
item.description: The American Museum of Natural History said it would repatriate hair clippings that must be returned to tribes under a 1990 federal law.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Christopher Gregory-Rivera for The New York Times
item.media:description: Data sheets from an anthropologist who collected hair samples from Native Americans in the 1890s are stored in boxes at the American Museum of Natural History in New York.
